revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Azumi people]][[fr:Peuple Azumi]][[id:Suku Azumi]][[it:Azumi (popolo)]][[ja:阿曇氏]][[ko:아즈미씨]][[ru:Адзуми (народ)]][[simple:Azumi people]]
{{Short description|Historical ethnic group inhabiting northern Kyūshū|qq=}} {{Infobox tribe | name = Azumi people | local name = | type = ''Warrior Clan, Seafarers, Farmers'' | image = [[File:Kyushu Region in Japan (extended).svg|frameless]] | image_size = | alt = | caption = [[Kyushu]] Region in Japan | ethnicity = Azumi | location = Northern [[Kyushu]] | varna = | descended = | population = | demonym = | branches = | language = {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} | religion = [[Shinto]]ism | surnames = Azumi no }} The {{Nihongo|'''Azumi'''|阿曇氏 or 安曇族|qq=}} were a warrior clan and tribe who originated during the [[Jōmon period]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, whose cultures and beliefs are considered to be one of Japan's earliest sea religions. Their existence dates back to the early 3rd – 7th centuries, when their extensive knowledge of navigation between waters made them the naval force of {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato Japan|1=Yamato period}} in Kyushu. They originally lived in Northern [[Kyushu]],<ref name=":1"/> especially in an area called {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|qid=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen|1=Chikuzen Province}}, now part of modern-day {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. Their knowledge and ability to use tidal changes, weather patterns and star constellations ensured their successful routing and voyaging on the sea during their regular exploration. In essence, the working of Azumi life was centred around their sea God, {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|qid=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} and their omnipotent deity ‘[[Azumi-no-isora|Isora]]’, who provided the Azumi people with a strong sense of spiritual guidance throughout their day-to-day life.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|last=Rambelli|first=F|title=The Sea and The Sacred in Japan|publisher=Bloomsbury Academic Publishing|year=2018|isbn=978-1350062870|location=Camden|pages=preface|qq=}}</ref> == History == The Azumi people were a tribe from Northern [[Kyushu]] in Southern Japan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=The Azumi Basin in Japan and Its Ancient People|url=http://voices.yahoo.com/the-azumi-basin-japan-its-ancient-people-8034946.html?cat=37|last=Andriyenko|first=L|date=15 March 2011|website=Web Archive|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131231151129/http://voices.yahoo.com/the-azumi-basin-japan-its-ancient-people-8034946.html?cat=37|archive-date=2013-12-31|access-date=18 May 2020|qq=}}</ref> They were the oldest group and leaders of the “Ama”, a group of people who navigated the waters of West Japan (Tsushima), Korea and China.<ref name=":02"/> They also performed trade with China and Korea and were responsible for the transport of iron from the Korean Peninsula to Northern Kyushu.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Yamada|first=S|title=The Spaciality of Awaji Island: An Essay regarding the Place of Gossa-Kaito Archaelogical Site and Environs|publisher=Gossa Walking Museum|location=Nishiwaki|qq=}}</ref> According to some historians, the Azumi were "the oldest known maritime force of [Japan's] emerging imperial state."<ref name=":02"/> The Azumi people were extremely skilled seafarers and their extensive knowledge of water currents, tidal changes, weather and star constellations is believed to have been passed down from generation to generation.<ref name=":02"/> Their ability to navigate the seas, especially at night, made them useful to the Japanese imperial government during the 3rd to early 5th centuries and they were effectively the naval force of {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato Japan|1=Yamato period}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |title=The Protocol of the Gods: A Study of the Kasuga Cult in Japanese History |date=1993 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=9780520910362 |page=32 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PI133z1ObW4C&pg=PA32 |access-date=25 May 2020|qq=}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|last=Palmer|first=E|title=Harima Fudoki: A Record of Ancient Japan Reinterpreted, Translated, Annotated with Commentary|publisher=Brill|year=2015|isbn=978-9004269378|location=Leiden|pages=144|qq=}}</ref> With the new responsibility and accountability accorded to them, the Azumi people became the biggest influence in controlling the various Inland Sea routes to Kyushu along the Inland Coast.<ref name=":02"/> In this role, it was the Grand General of the naval force who was in charge of protecting these seas. Azumi no Hirafu, a member of the Azumi tribe, was appointed this role as Grand General when the emperor sighted him along with a group of men swimming toward their boat on the Inland Sea. These men wore {{ill|Deer|en|Deer|qid=Q23390|lt=Deer|1=Deer}} antlers covering their heads, appearing as a close herd of deer on the seas. In the ancient beliefs of [[Shinto]], deer were believed to be spiritual messengers, or specifically ‘Messengers to God’. Today in Nara, the modern capital of Japan, this belief and symbolism of the deer has continued, as over one thousand deer freely roam the Kasuga Grand Shrine, remaining intrinsically linked with both the kami revered there and the descendants of the Azumi.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Dealing with Japan's Sacred Deer|url=https://www.patheos.com/blogs/pagantama/2017/08/05/japans-sacred-deer/|last=Manson|first=M|date=5 August 2017|website=Patheos|access-date=19 May 2020|qq=}}</ref> Before voyaging, the Azumi people often performed rituals of sea-worship to the oceanic deities as they believed this assisted them to establish control and territory in various regions of the sea. However, when they faced defeat at the Bay of Hakunsonko in 633, Azumi no Hirafu, along with other members of the Azumi tribe spread along to the various regions of Japan, with the majority leaving the straits of northern Kyushu.<ref name=":02"/> In these voyages, they became involved in other specialities and traditions. One of these roles included preparing and tasting imperial food,<ref name=":6">{{Cite book|last=Grapard|first=A. G.|title=The Protocol of the Gods: a study of the Kasuga Cult in Japanese History|publisher=University of California Press|year=1992|isbn=0520910362|location=Berkeley|pages=270|qq=}}</ref> otherwise known as “Saba”. Under the Japanese legal system of [[Ritsuryō]], they became affiliated with the department of the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|qid=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}} responsible for preparing the emperor's food, largely because of their historic role of providing seafood to the imperial state. The Azumi became prominently known for specialising in preparing imperial meals later in the clan's history as this formed as an additional part of the responsibility that they owed the imperial government.<ref name=":6" /><ref name=":02"/> Although some of the Azumi people stayed and continued the traditional life as sea goers,<ref name=":02"/> for those who decided to leave sea life, many ended up on the small island of Shinshu, where they became heavily involved in farming and agriculture. During these times, even though they were no longer seafarers, it is believed that they still maintained their intrinsic connection to their past ancestors through regularly praying to the deity Isora, thankful for the water which flowed from Kamikochi's mountains granting them successful crop irrigation.<ref name=":5" /> The Azumi people who did not choose to engage with the farming lifestyle in Shinshu ended up in the Azumi Basin. Here, they formed a small fishing village and developed their language, continuing to practice their core spirituality to the deity Isora. Over time, they became known as “the people who lived at the sea.”<ref name=":1" /> In the late 7th Century, the Azumi people also had a key role in maintaining peace for the Yamato Court's maritime and became core allies in warfare.<ref name=":02"/> However, this rapport became troubled during the ‘Tenumu Reign’ in 682. Emperor Tenumu invaded South Kyushu, but instead of inflicting war on the Yamato Court, they offered tokens of their gratitude in appreciation of the Azumi people's comradery and supposed obedience to them. This gesture confused the Yamato Court, and led them to doubt and question the loyalty of the Azumi People.<ref name=":02"/> The ruling dynasty banished the Azumi sometime before the 8th century.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Joseph |first1=FRank |title=Unlocking the Prehistory of America |date=2013 |publisher=Rosen Publishing Group |isbn=978-1477728062 |page=177 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wpPnAgAAQBAJ&pg=PA177 |access-date=25 May 2020|qq=}}</ref> Today, the places to which the Azumi people voyaged to have been named after their presence (Kakojima, Kagoshima, Kashima, Shikashima) and there are still traces of their existence remaining in the areas they commonly voyaged through (Islands of Tsushima Straits, Kyushu, Shikoku, Awaji Islands).<ref name=":02"/> == Origin == According to Japanese historians, they were of {{ill|Austronesian peoples|en|Austronesian peoples|qid=Q861225|lt=Austronesian|1=Austronesian peoples}} origin, related to the {{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|qid=Q1151764|lt=Hayato people|1=Hayato people}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=蝦夷とアテルイ|url=http://masakawai.suppa.jp/encartaAA/bunka/emisi/emisi.html|access-date=2019-03-26|website=masakawai.suppa.jp|qq=}}</ref><ref name=":02"/> They were in early contact with the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato period}}, providing important maritime trade links to the Japanese capital and influencing the court's military and diplomatic efforts at sea.<ref name=":02" /> Some Azumi migrated further inland, settling in what is now known as [[:en:Azumino, Nagano|Azumino]] Valley or {{ill|Azumi Basin|en|Azumi Basin|qid=Q4833082|lt=Azumi Basin|1=Azumi Basin}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine|url=https://www.azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine|access-date=2020-04-16|website=Explore Azumino!|language=en-US|qq=}}</ref> They also spread toward the imperial center, settling in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu province|1=Settsu Province}} around what is now {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Hōga|first1=Toshio|title=Wani shi : chūgoku kōnan kara kita watatsumizoku no nagare|last2=宝賀寿男|publisher=Aogakishuppan|year=2012|isbn=978-4-434-16411-8|language=ja|oclc=779877582|qq=}}</ref> Remnants of Azumi culture are also found on {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|qid=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island|1=Awaji Island}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Yamada|first=Sohei|title=The Spaciality of Awaji Island: A Essay regarding the Place of Gossa-Kaito Archaeological Site and Environs|url=http://www.gossa-walking-museum.net/en/about_gossa/spaciality.html|access-date=2020-04-16|website=Gossa Walking Museum|qq=}}{{Dead link|date=September 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes |qq=}}</ref> Being a leading tribe during the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}}, the Azumi held the common belief of a deity who would allow for safe navigation of rough seas if prayed to before embarking on a sea journey. This deity, known as ‘[[Azumi-no-isora|Azumi no Isora]]’ is placed at the centre of the Azumi tradition and is believed to embody the seas which surround Japan.<ref name=":02"/> ‘Azumi no Isora’ is also believed to emerge during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, around the same time as three similar {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|qid=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} deities: Sokotsu, Uwatsu and Ojin. These three deities, once representing the seas that surround Japan, were said to infuse into a single being known as Isora.<ref name=":02"/> The spirit of Isora is represented as a {{ill|seahorse|en|seahorse|qid=Q74363|lt=seahorse|1=Seahorse}},<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|last=Davis|first=F. H|title=Myths and Legends of Japan|publisher=Dover Publications|year=1912|isbn=1602063699|location=11 York|pages=331|qq=}}</ref> and is considered a symbol of purity, providing spiritual guidance to the Azumi people throughout their day-to-day life.<ref name=":02"/> Additionally, Azumi no Isora is suggested to be the primordial human ancestor as it was him that first performed the dance ritual known as “Sei-no-no” (also known as Seino and Kuwashi-O).<ref name=":02"/> The dance was believed to be performed in two parts with a dialogue intermission in between, spoken by two characters: the deity Isora and the goddess Toyo. In the dialogue, Isora is described to be an old man with white hair.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ito|first=S|title=Supernatural Elements in No Drama|publisher=University of London|year=1969|isbn=978-0-355-48561-5|location=London|pages=56–58|qq=}}</ref> “Sei-no-no” consists of four male dancers: two with small drums to accompany their movements and two without. All four were described to have a white cloth covering their face, dancing along in the night to the melody of flutes.<ref name=":02"/> This dance has been passed down through Azumi tribe generations as a ritualistic way to explore their connection to Azumi no Isora and express their praise. It was also performed with the shells of sea turtles by the ‘Urabe Diviners’ (the first adherents of Azumi no Isora) as a gateway to determine which days were safe for voyages to sea.<ref name=":02"/> Additionally, the Azumi people's kinship to the Urabe Diviners and their history is symbolised by three deity stones in the shape of a sea turtle.<ref name=":02"/> Today, there are multiple shrines which are dedicated to the Azumi People. There is one in Genkai Bay on [[:en:Shikanoshima Island|Shikanoshima Island]] dedicated to Azumi no Isora which contains sacred stones to represent the sea turtles, as well as over a thousand deer antlers to maintain their relationship with the deities.<ref name=":02"/> There is another on the north side of Japan known as the Hodaka shrine. This ritualistic shrine is thought to be a ‘mini-museum’, solely dedicated to preserving the rich culture and history of the Azumi People.<ref name=":02"/> == Folklore == When the Azumi migrated to and established themselves in the {{ill|Azumi Basin|en|Azumi Basin|qid=Q4833082|lt=Azumi Basin|1=Azumi Basin}} around 1000 years ago, the first folklore about them was recorded.<ref name=":1" /> It was here where the Azumi people made up legends to warn their children of the dangers associated with wandering near the water, telling stories of a golden koi fish leaping from the water to snatch children away if they were near a river or shore alone.<ref name=":1" /> An origin folktale exploring the emergence of the Azumi people and their ancestor-deities is known as “The Pyroclastic Birth of Takemikatsuchi”. This follows the story of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} and [[Izanagi]]; sacred deities of the Shinto religion. They were believed to be the divine beings who created the universe and formed the Island of Japan by churning it with their adorned spear.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book|last=Grapard|first=A. G.|title=The Protocol of the Gods: a study of the Kasuga Cult in Japanese History|publisher=University of California Press|year=1992|isbn=0520910362|location=Berkeley|pages=32|qq=}}</ref> However, their initial attempt at creating life was unsuccessful, with Izanami birthing a leech. It was believed to result from her disobedience in following the Japanese social construct of a woman – she initiated the sexual intercourse, a role assumed to be dominated by men.<ref name=":3" /> Although they eventually achieve success with their creation – birthing the Eight Islands of Japan and creating kami – their attempts lead to other unfortunate events, including the death of Izanami. This angers Izanagi to commit various tainted acts becoming decayed and polluted within himself.<ref name=":3" /> Eventually, Izanagi chooses to repent and attempts to clean and purify himself of the stain. This is when the kami of the Watatsumi and [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi]] appeared. These two kami are extremely important as they are believed to be the ancestors-deities of the Azumi tribe, playing a vital role in their emergence.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Uehara|first=T|title=The Dynamics of The Archetypes in Japanese Shinto Mythology|publisher=University of Southern California|year=1960|isbn=9781084372177|location=Los Angeles|pages=210, 472|qq=}}</ref> Additionally, these two kami, along with Isora became known to be worshipped by the Azumi people as they were associated with skill, strategy, and seafaring.<ref name=":3" /> Another folktale included the powers of their divine deity ‘Isora’. The Azumi generations detail the creation story of Isora and how his once hidden jewels located in a dragon palace on the seafloor were said to control the tides and weather patterns. They believed that the king dragon which resided in the palace did not show himself due to his ugly outward appearance; his body covered with {{ill|Abalone|en|Abalone|qid=Q4857439|lt=Abalone|1=Abalone}}, [[:en:barnacles|barnacles]] and {{ill|Shellfish|en|Shellfish|qid=Q6501235|lt=Shellfish|1=Shellfish}}. However, it is suggested that the ‘''kagura’'' dance possessed the ability to lure out the dragon, leading the Azumi people to his endless supply of ‘treasure’: divine knowledge of the sea.<ref name=":02"/> ‘''Kagura’'' directly translates to ‘god-performance’ is linked closely with the religion of Shinto. It is suggested that ''‘kagura’'' was similar, if not identical to the “Sei-no-no” dance,<ref name=":02"/> and has been passed down and carried through the many generations. Now, it involves a masked theatrical re-enactment of Japanese history, where the performers retell the origin stories of Shinto.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Asian Traditional Theatre & Dance|url=https://disco.teak.fi/asia/kagura-sacred-shinto-dances/|last=Miettinen|first=J. O.|date=2018|access-date=19 May 2020|qq=}}</ref> Today, shrines such as the Shikaumi-jinja Shrine Okitsu-gu on Shinkanoshima Island has become the basis for worshipping Isora as well as the basis for preserving these folktales for future generations.<ref name=":1" /> == Landmarks == === Myojin-Pond === [[File:Myojin_Pond_Nino-ike_01.JPG|thumb|"Mirror-like" reflection of Myojin Pond.|222x222px]][[File:Shikaumi-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|[[Shikaumi Shrine]], considered the ancestral shrine of the Azumi, in Fukuoka.]]The Myojin Pond in Kamikochi, Japan attracts both traditional descendants of the Azumi people, as well as tourists. It has a clear, mirror-like reflection, and is classified as one of the most revered places to worship the deities of the Azumi people.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Myojin-ike Pond|url=https://www.findnewjapan.com/nature/myojin-pond-%E6%98%8E%E7%A5%9E%E6%B1%A0/|last=Takeshi|date=3 October 2019|website=Find 11 Japan|access-date=19 May 2020|qq=}}{{Dead link|date=September 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes |qq=}}</ref> At the '''{{ill|Hotaka Shrine|en|Hotaka Shrine|qid=Q11597109|lt=Hotaka Shrine|1=Hotaka Shrine}}''' in Azumino City, there is an annual festival at which people express their gratitude for the gift of water. In the city, there is a traditional spot in which all three rivers (Azusa, Karasu and Nakabusa), as well as the water from the melted snow of Kamikochi's mountains, meet. Kamikochi mountain holds a great significance to the Azumi people as the water which flowed down this mountain was once used to irrigate their crops.<ref name=":5">{{Cite web|title=Azusa - Nature and Culture Flow as One in the Sacred River|url=http://www.kamikochi.org/history/asusa-nature-and-culture-flow-as-one-in-the-sacred-river-2-|last=Habington|first=Will|date=12 June 2012|website=Kamikochi|access-date=19 May 2020|qq=}}</ref> Using this water, the people of Japan perform the rituals known as “Omizu-tori” (taking water) and “Omizu-gaeshi” (returning water), whereby the water is deemed to rightfully return to the Myojin Pond.<ref name=":5" /> During this celebration, the Azumi People commemorate their direct connection to water and their gratitude for it as it has always assisted them – both in their history as skilful seafarers and crop farmers.<ref name=":02"/> Today, the Myojin Pond accommodates many visitors as the water that lays within is a reminder of the culture, tradition, and history of the Azumi people.<ref name=":1" /> === Shikaumi Jinja === {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} is home to what is considered the ancestral shrine of the Azumi, [[Shikaumi Shrine]]. The shrine is presided over by a priest descended from the Azumi clan, and its biennial ''gojinkosai'' ceremony is designated an {{ill|Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q2901957|lt=intangible cultural property|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}} by the prefecture.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Shikaumi Jinja Gojinkosai|url=http://www.dydo-matsuri.com/english/list/2015/shikaumi/|website=DyDo GROUP Matsuri of Japan|access-date=2020-04-16|archive-date=2020-01-31|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200131135054/http://www.dydo-matsuri.com/english/list/2015/shikaumi/|url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref> The shrine and its rituals make use of a deer motif, common to the historic Azumi people.<ref name=":02"/> == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan|qq=}} {{Watatsumi and Sumiyoshi Faiths|qq=}}
[[en:Azumi people]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Peuple Azumi]] [[id:Suku Azumi]] [[ja:阿曇氏]] [[ko:아즈미씨]] [[ru:Адзуми (народ)]]
[[simple:Azumi people]]
{{wikidata link|Q3377806}} [[Category:Clans of Kyūshū Region]] [[Category:Archaic words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:History of Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Azumi people]]
